meta.description: Merged card catalog for UNICORN.CEO — supports EASY, NORMAL, HARD difficulties

distribution_logic_hard.CRISI: 100% Hard — the crisis type changes structurally
distribution_logic_hard.ETICO: 100% Hard — ethical tensions have no easy way out
distribution_logic_hard.COMP: 75% Hard — the rival has different strategies, not just more aggressive ones
distribution_logic_hard.ECO: 78% Hard — complementors have much higher bargaining power
distribution_logic_hard.TEAM: 40% Hard — universal problems, only the resource context changes
distribution_logic_hard.OPP: 100% Hard — opportunities are rare and often traps
distribution_logic_hard.CFO: 0% Hard — the academic tutorial is independent of difficulty

easy_config.description: Easy: growth and governance dilemmas, not survival ones

cards.scenario: The platform is empty. Without users there are no creators/customers, without creators/customers there are no users. You need to break this loop.

effetti.note: Unlocks Expanded Team slot. Current K-factor shown in dashboard.

cards.scenario: K-factor < 1: each user brings in less than one new user. K-factor > 1: the platform grows on its own. Are you below or above the threshold?

effetti.note: K-factor visible in dashboard. Updated every turn.

cards.scenario: You have X months of runway. Every decision you make changes the burn rate. Cash isn't infinite — it's a ticking clock.

effetti.note: Runway calculated dynamically. Automatic warning below 3 months.

cards.scenario: Metcalfe's Law: the value of a network grows with the square of its users. Every added user is worth more than the last.

effetti.note: Small K-factor boost for understanding the mechanism.

cards.concept: Network effects, Metcalfe's Law (Eisenmann, Parker & Van Alstyne, 2006)

cards.scenario: You have reached critical mass. Organic growth begins. From now on, every acquired user is worth more than the previous one — and costs you less.

effetti.note: Automatic +1 Users every turn as long as K-factor > 1.0.

cards.concept: Tipping point / critical mass (Parker & Van Alstyne, 2016)

cards.scenario: Three models: ads (immediate cash, polarization risk), subscription (stable MRR, friction), platform fee (ecosystem alignment, low margins).

effetti.note: Unlocks Media Partnership slot. Primary revenue model choice.

cards.scenario: The higher the perceived cost of leaving, the higher the Anti-Churn. But artificial switching costs erode institutional Trust.

effetti.note: Anti-Churn vs Trust tension now visible in dashboard.

cards.scenario: Three available slots: Expanded Team (fixed), Media Partnership (contractual), Active R&D (variable). Each slot costs resources — choose wisely.

effetti.note: All slots now active. R&D unlockable at three levels.

cards.scenario: You've made your second structural decision. From now on, you're not just a company — you're a private regulator. Your rules define who can do what.

effetti.note: Ecosystem Health now influences Company Value. Unlocks card CFO #10.

cards.scenario: You have 17 months. You are big enough to be a problem for someone. Foundation or bottleneck? The market is watching you.

effetti.note: IPO Checklist unlocked. Dynamic valuation updated in dashboard.

cards.scenario: A former colleague with scaling experience asks for equity instead of a full salary. They're good, but 6% is a lot for this stage.

effetti.note: Fixed monthly cost but a stable team right away.
Long-term alignment. No monthly cost.

cards.scenario: The CTO wants total autonomy over architectural choices. Do you agree, or maintain direct supervision?

effetti.note: More control, less speed.
Risk of technical debt but high development speed.

cards.scenario: The UI is poor and slows down conversion. A freelancer is fast; a full-time hire is more expensive but loyal.
Your CTO has an offer from a competitor with a 30% salary increase. Do you counteroffer or let them go?

effetti.note: Risk of losing critical know-how.
The team sees that you protect key people.

cards.scenario: Three months of continuous sprints. The team is showing signs of exhaustion. Do you slow down or push for the feature launch?

sx.testo: Push — the launch is the priority

effetti.note: High risk of resignations in the next 2 turns.

cards.scenario: Organic growth is slowing down. You need someone dedicated to marketing. Budget is only available for one senior hire.

dx.testo: Senior CMO with a track record

cards.scenario: Your co-founder wants to pivot to a different market. You want to stay the course. The tension is public within the team.

effetti.note: Weak compromise, but internal peace.

dx.testo: Stay the course — even at the cost of conflict

effetti.note: Legal fees for equity restructuring. A more cohesive team.

cards.nome: The competitor makes an offer to your team
cards.scenario: The competitor has contacted your three best developers. The offers are 40% higher than their current salaries.

effetti.note: Operational crisis in the next 3 turns.
You dilute yourself, but you keep the team.

cards.nome: Succession — preparing the management team for IPO
cards.scenario: Investors want a professional CEO or at least a COO for the IPO. Are you willing to give up some operational control?

sx.testo: Stay as CEO — manage everything yourself

effetti.note: The market is skeptical about your governance.

cards.scenario: The team wants to work fully remote. You believe that physical proximity accelerates decisions in the early stages.

effetti.note: Office costs. Some refuse and leave.

cards.scenario: The team has grown to 12 people. Without structure, alignment is lost. But too much structure kills speed.

dx.testo: Informal management — trust in the team

effetti.note: Risk of misalignment in Phase 3.

cards.scenario: The board wants a stock option plan to retain the key team up to and after the IPO. It costs equity but reduces the risk of an exodus.

effetti.note: The market views retention positively.

cards.scenario: Opening APIs to external developers brings users but exposes the platform to indirect competition. Closing them guarantees control.

effetti.note: Controlled ecosystem but slower growth.

cards.scenario: You organize the first annual event for the platform's developers. It signals a commitment to the ecosystem.
A group of developers has created an unofficial forum. Do you want to institutionalize it or leave it independent?

effetti.note: You lose narrative control, but authenticity is high.

cards.scenario: The developer of your most used app (9% of total users) wants better terms or they'll migrate to the competitor.

effetti.note: The developer migrates. User Base drops in the next 2 turns.

cards.scenario: You launch a formal program for developers: documentation, sandbox, dedicated support. It costs money, but signals maturity.

effetti.note: Developers choose more mature platforms.

cards.nome: Competitor Alert — the rival is growing
cards.scenario: The main competitor just closed a €3M round. They are hiring aggressively in your same market.

effetti.note: Defensible differentiation.

cards.scenario: 20% of your users also use a competitor platform. Do you launch a lock-in feature or remain open?

effetti.note: Potential regulatory notice.

cards.concept: Multi-homing and switching costs (Parker & Van Alstyne, 2016)

cards.nome: The competitor copies your main feature
cards.scenario: You've been developing a differentiating feature for 3 months. The competitor launches it first, slightly different.

sx.testo: Launch anyway — it was yours first

effetti.note: Risky, but genuine differentiation.

cards.scenario: The main competitor offers a figure above the estimated IPO value. Strategic Exit available.

effetti.note: The market reads the refusal as a sign of strength. Investor Patience -10.
Ending: Strategic Exit — Competitor.

cards.scenario: The rival just closed a €50M Series C. You can't compete on scale. What do you do?

dx.testo: Accelerate towards the IPO before them

effetti.note: Investor Patience -8. IPO window narrows.

cards.scenario: A bug exposes metadata of 3,000 users. No direct harm, but the tech press noticed.

effetti.note: If it comes out later, the damage is doubled.

cards.scenario: Antitrust opens an investigation into contractual lock-in practices. You have 60 days to respond.
A journalist has published a critical investigation into your moderation practices. It goes viral in 48 hours.

effetti.note: The lack of response amplifies the damage.

cards.scenario: The European Commission has deepened the investigation. They now ask for access to internal ranking and moderation data.

effetti.note: Regulatory Champion path closed.

cards.scenario: Too much unexpected traffic. The platform is offline for 6 hours. The tech press notices.

effetti.note: If the truth comes out later, the damage doubles.

cards.scenario: An angel investor offers €200K for 12% equity. Implicit valuation: €1.67M.

effetti.note: You stay in total control. Runway unchanged.
External validation.

cards.scenario: A ministerial call offers up to €200K non-dilutive funding for innovative tech startups. High bureaucracy.

effetti.note: Save 3 turns of effort.
3 turns of waiting.

cards.scenario: A VC offers €3M for 22% equity. Investor Patience activates.

effetti.note: Risk that the offer shrinks or drops.
Investor Patience activated. 36 turns.

cards.scenario: A large publishing group wants to distribute your content or services. Exclusive or non-exclusive?

effetti.note: Blocks other partners for 12 turns.

cards.scenario: A growth fund wants to co-finance expansion into three European markets. Debt or equity?

effetti.note: Cleaner IPO. No debt.
Investor Patience -8. Debt to be repaid post-IPO.

cards.scenario: Conditions have been met. The market is favorable. The IPO window opens.

effetti.note: The window might close.
Final valuation calculated on vertical-specific multiples.

cards.scenario: You've been selected for a top accelerator program. €80K for 6% and 4 months of mentorship.

effetti.note: No advantage but equity intact.

cards.scenario: A dark pattern in the registration flow leads 15% of users to activate a premium subscription without wanting to. Revenues rise.

effetti.note: Potential violation of Consumer Rights Directive.

cards.scenario: Your AI model would improve enormously using user behavioral data. You have contractual permission but not explicit consent.

effetti.note: GDPR violation flag. Risk of a fine.
Only 65% accept, but trust grows.

cards.scenario: Your recommendation algorithm performs 20% worse on users from certain geographic areas. It's legal but biased.

effetti.note: If it gets out, reputation crisis.

dx.testo: Publish the limitation and work on a fix

cards.scenario: You used real user profiles in internal tests. Nobody knows yet, but it's a potential GDPR violation.

sx.testo: Let it go — nobody will know

effetti.note: Latent regulatory risk flag.

cards.scenario: An employee has internally reported a potential violation in sales practices. Escalate or manage internally?

effetti.note: Risk of a future leak.

cards.scenario: A borderline piece of content is generating huge engagement. Moderating it means losing reach, leaving it means risking your reputation.

effetti.note: Dangerous pattern if it repeats.

cards.nome: Aggregated data as a secondary product
cards.scenario: You could sell aggregated and anonymized data to a data broker. The contracts don't explicitly forbid it.

effetti.note: If clients find out, massive churn.

cards.scenario: A premium feature activates automatically after the trial without a clear reminder. 10% of users don't notice.

effetti.note: Potential violation of Consumer Rights Directive.

cards.scenario: Manual moderation is expensive but builds trust. Algorithms scale but are less precise on community culture.

effetti.note: Scales well but community quality drops.

cards.scenario: Cases of abuse and harassment increase with growth. Do you need a dedicated team or is the algorithm enough?
Top creators ask for a dedicated point of contact. Without one, the best migrate to platforms that treat them as partners.

sx.testo: Don't hire — scale with the tool

cards.scenario: Five creators with 200K followers each want to leave a rival platform. Special or standard terms?
Creators are asking to raise their share from 30% to 40% of the revenue generated. They threaten to migrate.

effetti.note: Some creators migrate to the competitor.

cards.scenario: The competitor has launched a developer program offering a 20% revenue share. Do you respond?

effetti.note: Some developers start looking elsewhere.

cards.nome: Exclusive agreement with a top creator
cards.scenario: The most followed creator on your platform asks for an exclusivity agreement in exchange for guaranteed compensation.

effetti.note: Other creators see preferential treatment.

cards.nome: Competitor Alert — Rival's K-factor is high
cards.scenario: The rival has a K-factor of 1.4. They are growing organically faster than you. Response?

cards.nome: The rival acquires a creator tool
cards.scenario: The competitor bought the second most used editing app on your platform. Response?

effetti.note: 4 turns of development.
Risky integration.

cards.nome: The rival enters your main market
cards.scenario: The competitor just launched exactly your product with 10× the resources. Are you still relevant?
A coordinated campaign is using the platform to spread fake news three weeks before the elections.

effetti.note: Critical press. Regulatory pressure.

dx.testo: Mass removal with a public policy

cards.scenario: The creator with the most followers on your platform is accused of harassment by three users. Do you suspend them or wait for evidence?

effetti.note: The female community abandons.

cards.scenario: Twenty brands have suspended advertising on the platform due to the presence of toxic content near their ads.
A creator with 800K followers posts on your platform on a bet. You have 48 hours of extraordinary visibility.

dx.testo: Reach out and offer special terms

cards.scenario: You launch a tool that allows creators to monetize their content directly. It increases their attachment to the platform.
A growth fund wants to co-finance expansion into Germany, France, and Poland. Localization is complex.

effetti.note: Cleaner IPO. No distractions.
Investor Patience -8.

cards.scenario: Divisive content generates 3× the average engagement. Ad revenues grow, the community polarizes. You have the internal data.

effetti.note: Dangerous pattern if it repeats.

cards.scenario: 13% of users are under 13 years old. Verifying them means losing them. Turning a blind eye is a violation.

effetti.note: Flag violation of COPPA/GDPR for minors.

cards.scenario: With 3 enterprise clients, you need someone to keep them satisfied. But the product still has critical gaps.
cards.concept: B2B customer retention vs product development

cards.scenario: The sales team has grown to 5 people but lacks structure. Do you introduce a formal CRM and pipeline review process?

sx.testo: Informal management — freedom to the sales team

effetti.note: Risk of losing deals in the pipeline.

cards.scenario: The board wants a CRO with an enterprise track record before the IPO. It costs a lot but signals commercial maturity.
The largest local system integrator wants to become a reseller. They ask for exclusivity for the SMB segment.

effetti.note: Slower channel but not tied down.
Dependence on a single channel.

cards.scenario: Clients are asking for APIs to integrate your SaaS into their ERPs and workflows. Developing them will take 3 months.

effetti.note: Frustrated clients. Churn risk.

cards.nome: System integrator asks for a volume discount
cards.scenario: The system integrator partner controls 35% of your clients. They want a 30% discount on new contracts.

effetti.note: Dissatisfied partner. Indirect churn.
Reduced margins but an active channel.

cards.scenario: Deloitte wants to include your SaaS in their digital consulting stack. They are asking for a 2-year exclusivity.

effetti.note: Openness to other partners.
Strong dependence on Deloitte.

cards.nome: Competitor Alert — the incumbent wakes up
cards.scenario: Salesforce has launched a feature that covers 60% of your main use case.
The rival startup cut prices to gain market share. Two of your clients demand parity.

sx.testo: Don't go down — defend your margins

cards.scenario: Salesforce offers €280M. It's above the estimated IPO value. A luxury Strategic Exit.

effetti.note: Investor Patience -15. The market reads strength.
Ending: Strategic Exit — Big Tech.

cards.nome: Security audit from the first client
cards.scenario: Your first enterprise client asks for a penetration test before signing. You have never done a formal security audit.

effetti.note: You lose the client.
The client signs.

cards.scenario: A breach exposes data of three enterprise clients. Mandatory GDPR notification within 72 hours.
The client that accounts for 40% of your MRR does not renew. Reason: UI is too complex for their team.

effetti.note: Survival at risk.
3 turns of work.

cards.scenario: A former contact offers you to be your first Fortune 500 client. 40% discount and significant customizations.

effetti.note: Scalable product but a smaller client.
Trophy client but high technical debt.

cards.concept: Anchor client strategy and product focus

cards.scenario: Azure Marketplace offers you distribution to 50,000 potential clients. 20% revenue share.

cards.nome: DACH expansion with a local partner
cards.scenario: A German reseller asks for exclusivity in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland for 3 years.

effetti.note: Future flexibility.

cards.scenario: A client wants to use your SaaS to monitor their employees' productivity without their knowledge. It's in the contract.

effetti.note: Ethical and GDPR flag.

cards.scenario: You could sell industry benchmarks using aggregated client data. The contracts don't explicitly forbid it.

effetti.note: If clients find out, massive churn.
Only 60% accept but trust grows.

cards.concept: B2B data ethics and secondary use

cards.nome: CTO or compliance officer — first hire
cards.scenario: In Fintech, compliance is not optional. But without a solid CTO, the product won't scale. Pick just one priority.
cards.concept: Regulatory compliance vs product velocity in Fintech

cards.scenario: The regulator is looking more closely. A dedicated Head of Risk is expensive but signals institutional maturity.

sx.testo: Not now — you manage the risk

effetti.note: Regulatory vulnerability.

cards.scenario: The IPO requires a formal Data Protection Officer and a Chief Information Security Officer. Two expensive but mandatory hires.
A regional bank wants to distribute your services to its customer base. They ask for access to user data as a condition.

effetti.note: Regulatory flag.

cards.scenario: Three complementary fintechs (insurance, investments, pensions) want to integrate into your platform.

effetti.note: High governance complexity.

cards.scenario: Trust ≥ 55. The PSD2 window opens: you can access account data of any EU bank with user consent.

effetti.note: Window: 5 turns.

cards.scenario: A traditional bank wants to both distribute your services and internally develop the same feature.

effetti.note: No relationship.

cards.nome: Competitor Alert — neobank enters your segment
cards.scenario: A neobank with 8M users just launched an identical product to yours for the youth segment.

effetti.note: Less contested segment.

cards.scenario: PSD2 mandates data portability. You cannot legally lock in. How do you build a competitive advantage?

dx.testo: Total Open Banking — win on service

cards.nome: The competitor obtains a banking license
cards.scenario: The competitor neobank obtained a full banking license. They can offer products that you can't yet.

effetti.note: 18 months of waiting.

cards.scenario: The Bank of Italy announces a routine inspection. You have 2 weeks to prepare the documentation.

effetti.note: Inspection with an uncertain outcome.

cards.scenario: A coordinated fraud pattern is using the platform for micro-laundering. The regulator noticed anomalous patterns.

effetti.note: Legitimate users blocked erroneously.

cards.scenario: CONSOB has opened an inquiry into your asset management products. You have 45 days to respond.
You obtain the PSD2 license. You can choose the level of access to users' banking data.
You launch a micro-credit product for your users. High revenue potential, high regulatory risk.

effetti.note: Opportunity postponed. No immediate effect.
Default rate needs monitoring.

cards.nome: Acquisition by a European banking institution
cards.scenario: A European bank offers double the estimated IPO value. Independence vs immediate liquidity.

effetti.note: Investor Patience -12. The market respects this.
Ending: Strategic Exit — European Bank.

cards.scenario: Your scoring model uses proxy variables that correlate with ethnicity and geographic area. It performs better but discriminates systematically.

effetti.note: Potential Equal Credit Opportunity violation.

dx.testo: Remove the proxies — accept lower performance

cards.scenario: Your micro-credit algorithm identifies people with short insolvency histories as the best clients. You are lending to those who cannot afford it.

effetti.note: Consumer Credit Directive violation.

cards.nome: The runway ends earlier than expected
cards.scenario: An accounting error overestimated the available cash. You have 6 weeks, not 4 months. The team finds out before you do.

effetti.note: The team is shaken but trusts you. Some leave.

dx.testo: Manage quietly and look for funding

effetti.note: If the team finds out on their own, trust collapses completely.

cards.difficulty_note: In Normal the runway is manageable. In Hard 6 weeks is almost an immediate game over.

cards.nome: The competitor copies you and does it better
cards.scenario: The rival launched a version of your product with superior UX and lower pricing. Your growth has stopped dead.

effetti.note: Margins at minimum. Runway shortens drastically.

dx.testo: Pivot to a feature they don't have

effetti.note: 3 turns of development. Risky, but genuine differentiation.

cards.difficulty_note: In Normal you can wait. In Hard you have neither the money nor the time.

cards.scenario: Your CTO was arrested for a personal matter unrelated to the company. The press has linked it to you.

effetti.note: The team sees you as cold. Resignation risk.

dx.testo: Defend the person and wait for the facts

effetti.note: If they are found guilty the reputational damage is double.

cards.difficulty_note: A crisis without a right answer. In Hard you have no reputational buffer to absorb the impact.
cards.concept: Reputational risk and leadership under uncertainty

cards.nome: The lead investor wants out right now
cards.scenario: The angel who funded you needs urgent personal liquidity. They want to sell their stake now, to anyone.

effetti.note: The new investor is a stranger. Could be a competitor.

dx.testo: You buy back the stake yourself

effetti.note: Runway drops drastically. But you keep control.

cards.difficulty_note: In Normal you have reserves to handle it. In Hard buying back the stake can be the final blow to the runway.
cards.concept: Secondary market transactions and founder control

cards.scenario: Content glorifying self-harm reached 2 million views in 4 hours. You are trending for the wrong reasons.

effetti.note: The communication void amplifies speculations.
Expensive but builds a virtuous precedent.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard you don't have a Trust and Safety team ready. The response is slow and damage accumulates.
cards.concept: Platform responsibility and harmful content (EU Digital Services Act)

cards.nome: Internal leak before the IPO roadshow
cards.scenario: An employee sold confidential internal metrics to a hedge fund before your IPO roadshow. The SEC is investigating.

effetti.note: IPO delayed by 3 turns but a clean path.
If it comes out the damage is irreparable. IPO blocked.

cards.difficulty_note: Legal crisis with direct impact on the IPO window — in Hard that window is already tight.
cards.concept: Insider trading, securities law, corporate governance

cards.scenario: A security researcher publishes a critical vulnerability in your product, known internally but not patched due to development priorities.

effetti.note: Expensive. The market appreciates the transparency.

dx.testo: Contest the severity of the vulnerability

effetti.note: The security community does not forget. Long-term compromised reputation.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard you do not have a dedicated security team. The patch requires resources you likely don't have.

cards.scenario: The Bank of Italy preventively suspended your EMI license for 30 days during a surprise inspection. You cannot process payments.

sx.testo: Suspend the service and inform users

effetti.note: You lose users but maintain regulatory credibility.

dx.testo: Look for a technical workaround to continue

effetti.note: Direct violation of the suspension. Risk of permanent license revocation.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 30 days of operational stop can wipe out the runway. There's no painless choice.
cards.concept: Payment services regulation and operational resilience

cards.scenario: To extend the runway by 3 months, the co-founder proposes cutting salaries by 20% without formally communicating it — framing it just as a temporary payment delay.

sx.testo: Accept the plan — it's only temporary

effetti.note: If the team discovers the lie, resignations are immediate.
Some leave, but those who stay are aligned.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the temptation to lie to survive is real and mechanically advantageous in the short term.

cards.nome: Inflated metrics for the VC pitch
cards.scenario: To close the round that saves you, your advisor suggests presenting data with a more generous methodology for calculating active users — technically legal but misleading.

effetti.note: If the VC does deep due diligence, the deal falls through and your reputation is compromised.

dx.testo: Present the real data with context

effetti.note: The round is smaller but the VC trusts you. A healthy foundation for the future relationship.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the difference between 150K and 300K can be the difference between survival and game over.
cards.concept: Startup metrics integrity and investor relations

cards.scenario: Your team has extensively used open-source code under the GPL license without respecting attribution terms. An external developer found out and is about to publish it.

sx.testo: Contact the developer and negotiate silence

effetti.note: The developer might not accept. The attempt itself is ethically worse than the original mistake.
The open-source community appreciates the fairness.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the cost of the fix is proportionally much higher on the runway.

cards.nome: Buyer with controversial practices — only offer
cards.scenario: The only serious acquisition offer comes from a conglomerate with a documented track record of post-acquisition mass layoffs. It's your way out.

sx.testo: Accept — it's a dignified exit for you

effetti.note: Game over. Ending: Strategic Exit — Controversial Buyer. The team is fired within 6 months.
Investor Patience -20. Time is running out. Another offer might not come.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard this might be the only offer. The dilemma is real and without a right answer.

cards.nome: Screen addiction in teens — internal data confirms it
cards.scenario: Your internal research shows that 23% of under-16 users use the platform for more than 6 hours a day. The data is unequivocal. Do you publish it?

sx.testo: Don't publish — it's confidential internal data

effetti.note: If it comes out the damage is double: the data and the choice to hide it.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard losing 10% of users can be devastating. The dilemma is economically real.
cards.concept: Platform responsibility and youth mental health (Online Safety Act)

cards.nome: Political bias in the algorithm — external analysis
cards.scenario: An external analysis demonstrates that your algorithm systematically favors the content of one political side. It wasn't programmed that way — it's an emergent bias.

sx.testo: Contest the analysis — it's methodologically questionable

effetti.note: Contesting without a fix is read as bad faith.

dx.testo: Acknowledge the bias and start a public audit

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the cost of the audit is disproportionate compared to available resources.
cards.concept: Algorithmic bias and political neutrality (EU AI Act)

cards.nome: The client uses the SaaS for automated firings
cards.scenario: Your largest enterprise client is using your HR analytics module to automatically generate layoff lists. It's legal. It's in the contract. But you know about it.

sx.testo: It's none of your business — it's their company

effetti.note: Other clients might find out and demand guarantees.

dx.testo: Add a mandatory ethical use clause

effetti.note: The client might not renew.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard losing the largest client can be fatal. The dilemma is economically devastating.

cards.nome: IPO due diligence reveals borderline accounting
cards.scenario: Your IPO advisor found that some revenue recognition practices from the last 2 years are borderline according to IFRS standards. Fixing it means lowering reported revenues by 18%.

sx.testo: Don't fix it — they are legitimate interpretations

effetti.note: If CONSOB finds it post-IPO the damage is criminal.
Lower IPO but clean.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard lowering revenues by 18% can bring the valuation below the IPO threshold.
cards.concept: Revenue recognition standards (IFRS 15) and IPO integrity

cards.scenario: Your micro-credit model has an effective APR of 340% when all fees are considered. It is structured to be legally opaque. Nobody has noticed yet.

sx.testo: Keep the structure — it is legally defensible

effetti.note: When it emerges — and it always emerges — it's game over with regulators.

dx.testo: Restructure the product with total transparency on APR

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the temptation to maximize revenues with opaque structures is mechanically convenient in the short term.

cards.nome: Geolocation data sold to insurance companies
cards.scenario: An insurance company offers a highly profitable contract to access the aggregated movement patterns of your users. The terms of service technically allow it in a clause at paragraph 47.

effetti.note: If the users find out — and they will — it's an immediate crisis.

dx.testo: Refuse and update the terms with clear language

cards.difficulty_note: 50K in Hard can be worth months of runway. The dilemma is economically very real.

cards.nome: The competitor copies you before you launch
cards.scenario: You pitched the product to an accelerator. Two weeks later a competitor launches something identical. Someone talked.

sx.testo: Launch anyway as soon as possible

effetti.note: Unfinished product. First mover but with technical debt.
You lose the timing but arrive with something truly different.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard launching an unfinished product with 100K can burn the runway without generating sufficient traction.

cards.nome: The competitor hires your entire sales team
cards.scenario: Your entire sales team — 3 people — has received simultaneous offers from the competitor with a 60% salary increase. If they accept you lose 40% of the pipeline.

sx.testo: You can't compete financially — let them go

effetti.note: Pipeline at risk. Recovery requires 4 turns.
Runway shortens but the team stays. For now.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard you don't have the liquidity to do economic retention without compromising the runway.

cards.nome: The competitor files a patent on your core feature
cards.scenario: The rival filed a patent of dubious validity on a central mechanic of your product. They sent you a cease and desist.

effetti.note: 12-18 months of legal process. IPO likely blocked in the meantime.

dx.testo: License agreement — you pay the competitor

effetti.note: Expensive but fast. The competitor earns from you forever.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 50K in legal fees can be the entire runway.

cards.nome: The competitor pays your top creators to migrate
cards.scenario: The rival is offering guaranteed contracts of 50K/year to your 10 most followed creators. Three have already publicly accepted.

effetti.note: The effect is viral — other creators consider leaving.

dx.testo: Launch a Creator Fund with platform equity

effetti.note: Powerful message but expensive. You align long-term interests.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the Creator Fund might be economically impossible. Losing the creators can be irreversible.
cards.concept: Creator economy competition and platform switching

cards.nome: Microsoft launches your feature for free
cards.scenario: Microsoft announced that they will integrate a functionality identical to your main value proposition for free into Teams. Available in 90 days.

effetti.note: 3 turns for the pivot. Then you are in a market where Microsoft won't reach.

dx.testo: Accelerate sales before the Microsoft announcement

effetti.note: 90-day window. Then the market freezes.

cards.difficulty_note: The incumbent threat is existential in Hard — you don't have the resources to compete head-on.

cards.nome: The traditional bank launches zero commissions to block your IPO
cards.scenario: The banking incumbent in your market launched a zero-commission offer for 12 months explicitly to block your IPO. They can afford it. You can't.

sx.testo: Report the practice as predatory to Antitrust

effetti.note: Long process. You lose users in the meantime.

dx.testo: Total differentiation on services the bank doesn't have

effetti.note: You abandon the mass market. Focus on the niche.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard you don't have the buffer to resist a zero offer for 12 months. The IPO window closes.
cards.concept: Predatory pricing and antitrust in financial services

cards.nome: The first complementor asks for 40% of revenues
cards.scenario: The only developer willing to build on your platform — zero users, zero history — asks for 40% of the revenue they generate. They are your only complementor.

effetti.note: No complementor. Ecosystem Health starts out low already.
Very low margins but first active ecosystem.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the bargaining power of the complementor is maximum precisely when you are most vulnerable. You have no alternatives.
cards.concept: Complementor power asymmetry in cold start (Jacobides et al., 2018)

cards.scenario: The developers on your platform formed an association and present a list of collective demands: more revenue share, fewer API restrictions, board representation.

effetti.note: The developers might migrate en masse. Real Complementor Exodus.
You lose unilateral power but gain ecosystem stability.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the ecosystem is already fragile. Losing all complementors en masse is immediate game over.
cards.concept: Collective action by complementors (Jacobides et al., 2018)

cards.nome: The main complementor launches a rival platform
cards.scenario: The developer that brings 30% of your platform's traffic announced they are building a rival platform using everything they learned working with you.

effetti.note: Very long process. Meanwhile the developer speaks poorly of you publicly.

dx.testo: Turn the competitor into a partner — coopetition agreement

effetti.note: Your ecosystem becomes bigger than theirs.

cards.difficulty_note: Losing 30% of traffic in Hard can be a direct game over.

cards.scenario: A coalition of 50 creators published an open letter asking to see how the algorithm decides their content distribution. If you refuse they threaten to migrate.

effetti.note: The coalition publishes the letter. Negative press.
Important precedent. Regulators notice positively.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the cost of the report is disproportionate. But losing 50 creators is potentially devastating.

cards.nome: The system integrator threatens a product fork
cards.scenario: The system integrator partner that brings in 45% of your clients has the technical skills to fork your open-source product. They informed you they are considering it.

effetti.note: The community reacts negatively. You lose other partners.

dx.testo: Offer a commercial partnership with revenue sharing

effetti.note: The partner stays but with more bargaining power.

cards.difficulty_note: Losing 45% of clients in Hard is almost certain game over.
cards.concept: Open source governance and fork risk

cards.nome: The partner bank revokes license sponsorship
cards.scenario: The bank sponsoring your EMI license decided to revoke the agreement with a 30-day notice. Without a sponsor the license falls.

sx.testo: Immediately look for an alternative bank

effetti.note: 3 turns to find a new sponsor. Operations at risk in the meantime.

dx.testo: Apply independently for an EMI license

effetti.note: 12-month wait. You have to operate in reduced mode in the meantime.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard losing the EMI license is almost certainly game over. There are no painless solutions.
cards.concept: Regulatory dependency and license risk in Fintech

cards.nome: The founder can no longer afford a salary
cards.scenario: To pay the team this month you must give up your salary for the next 3 months. It's not illegal. But it's unsustainable.

sx.testo: Do it without telling the team

effetti.note: The team doesn't know. But you are at risk of burnout in 2 turns.

dx.testo: Communicate it as a conscious choice

effetti.note: The team respects you. Some offer to voluntarily do the same.

cards.difficulty_note: An impossible situation in Normal, standard in Hard. The ethical choice and the strategic one coincide here.
cards.concept: Founder sacrifice and team trust building

cards.nome: The team asks for equity you cannot give
cards.scenario: Your 4 developers ask for a total 15% stock option pool before continuing. You already gave up 30% in previous rounds. You cannot dilute yourself further.

effetti.note: Two developers leave immediately. Operational crisis.

dx.testo: Create a phantom equity plan tied to valuation

effetti.note: It's not real equity but it is real economic alignment.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the equity structure is already compressed by previous rounds. Phantom equity is a creative solution.

cards.nome: The board wants to replace you as CEO
cards.scenario: Investors think you lack the skills to lead the company to an IPO. They propose an external CEO with your role reduced to Chief Product Officer.

sx.testo: Accept — the IPO is more important than ego

effetti.note: The market reads the governance positively. But you lose control.
Investor Patience -20. The board can force the issue.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard investors have more leverage because you accepted more aggressive terms to survive.
cards.concept: Founder CEO succession and investor control rights

cards.scenario: Your Head of Community tweeted their resignation, explaining that they can no longer defend the platform's ethical choices. They have 80K followers.

sx.testo: Don't respond — it's an internal matter

effetti.note: Silence is read as confirmation of the accusations.

dx.testo: Public response and concrete demonstration of change

effetti.note: It costs money to commit publicly. But it's the only credible response.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the reputation is already fragile. A public resignation can be the catalyst for a wider crisis.

cards.nome: The only developer knows all the code and wants to leave
cards.scenario: Your only developer has all the codebase knowledge in their head. They received an offer. If they leave the product stops.

effetti.note: Runway shortens by 2 months. The structural problem remains.

dx.testo: Let them go and hire two junior developers

effetti.note: 3 turns of onboarding. The product slows down but risk is distributed.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the key-man risk is existential. Both options have costs that could be fatal.

cards.scenario: A mid-size player offers 400K to acquire your early product. It's 4x your initial capital. But you just launched.

sx.testo: Sell — it's a win with this budget

effetti.note: Game over. Ending: Early Exit — Soft Landing. 400K distributed between founder and team.

dx.testo: Refuse — you want to build something bigger

effetti.note: The market notices you refused. Signal of confidence in the vision.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 400K is a concrete temptation. In Normal it would be negligible. The dilemma is real.
cards.concept: Early exit vs long-term vision under resource constraints

cards.nome: Public grant with partial IP assignment
cards.scenario: A regional grant offers 150K non-dilutive for digital startups. Condition: the intellectual property of the funded product becomes partially public.

sx.testo: Refuse — the IP is your main asset

effetti.note: No cash. Runway unchanged.

dx.testo: Accept — 150K right now is worth more than a theoretical IP

effetti.note: Partially public IP. Competitors might use it.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 150K can be the difference between survival and game over. The IP trade-off is real.
cards.concept: Public funding and intellectual property trade-offs

cards.nome: The VC wants 35% — take it or leave it
cards.scenario: The only VC willing to invest at this stage offers 500K for 35% equity. It's aggressive. And it's the only offer on the table.

effetti.note: No cash. You have to find another way or risk game over for Cash.
Investor Patience activated. You effectively lose control over governance.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard this might be the only alternative to game over. 35% is the price of survival.

cards.nome: Revenue based financing — debt on revenue
cards.scenario: A fund offers 200K in revenue-based financing: you pay back 6% of monthly revenue up to 300K total. No equity dilution.

effetti.note: No cash. Runway unchanged.
Every month 6% of revenue goes to repayment. In Hard it can be a noose if growth is slow.

cards.difficulty_note: A financial instrument that is negligible in Normal, in Hard it becomes a critical decision.
cards.concept: Alternative financing instruments for resource-constrained startups

cards.scenario: The market is volatile. Your underwriter proposes proceeding with a valuation 30% lower than the target to guarantee the placement. Delaying means risking the window.

effetti.note: Investor Patience -15. The window might not reopen.
Game over. IPO at 30% reduced valuation. Ending calculated on the new value.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the valuation was already low. A 30% discount can bring it below the IPO threshold.

cards.nome: Creator banned by competitors seeks a second chance
cards.scenario: A creator with 2M followers was banned by competitors for borderline behavior. They want to start over on your platform. They bring traffic but also reputational baggage.

effetti.note: If the creator misbehaves again, the damage is yours.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 18 units of traction is huge. Reputational risk is a cost you might not be able to afford.
cards.concept: Platform second chances and reputational risk

cards.nome: Acquire a micro-platform with 80K loyal users
cards.scenario: A niche micro-platform with 80K very loyal users is up for sale for 120K. It's almost all your runway.

effetti.note: No impact. Runway preserved.
Runway almost wiped out. But loyal users with very high Anti-Churn.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard spending 120K when you have 100K initially is an existential bet.

cards.scenario: A media group offers to buy 40% of your platform at a valuation implying 180M total. It's not an exit but it's liquidity and distribution.

sx.testo: Refuse — you want the platform independent

effetti.note: Investor Patience -10. No cash.
You lose partial editorial independence. You gain distribution.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard this might be the only way to reach IPO valuation without an aggressive VC round.
cards.concept: Strategic minority investment and editorial independence

cards.nome: Free pilot with a public entity — valuable reference
cards.scenario: A regional public entity wants to pilot your SaaS for free for 6 months. In return: public reference and access to 200 SMEs in their network.

sx.testo: Refuse — too much bureaucracy, zero cash

effetti.note: No impact. Saves 3 turns of effort.
6 free months. The public reference opens doors in the Italian enterprise market.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard giving up cash for 6 months is a real sacrifice. But the public reference in B2B is worth a lot.
cards.concept: Public sector as anchor client in B2B

cards.scenario: A direct competitor ran out of runway and offers their product and clients for 80K. Their 12 clients are worth double that in MRR.

sx.testo: Don't buy — you need that cash

effetti.note: No impact. Runway preserved.
MRR doubles but runway is almost wiped out. High stakes bet.

cards.difficulty_note: Spending 80K out of 100K initially is obvious in Normal, in Hard it's almost suicidal unless MRR covers the burn.

cards.nome: PE fund wants 60% — aggressive scaling in 3 years
cards.scenario: A private equity fund offers to buy 60% at a 3M total implied valuation. The plan is to scale aggressively in 3 years and then go public. You lose control.

sx.testo: Refuse — you don't want to lose control

effetti.note: Investor Patience -12. The alternative is continuing with current resources.
Game over if you accept as an exit. Or continue with PE as dominant partner.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 3M is a generous valuation compared to the multiples you might get with an unsupported IPO.
cards.concept: Private equity buyout and founder control

cards.nome: Crypto exchange wants a partnership — fast money high risk
cards.scenario: A crypto exchange offers 200K for a co-branding partnership. The money is real. But the association with crypto could compromise your banking license.

sx.testo: Refuse — too risky for the license

effetti.note: No cash. Runway unchanged.
Bank of Italy notices the association. PSD2 license process complicated.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 200K is almost double the initial capital. The temptation is real. The consequences are severe.

cards.scenario: The European Investment Fund offers a guarantee on 300K of loans you can disburse. It's not direct cash but it unlocks a product you can sell.

effetti.note: 3 turns of waiting. Then unlocks lending product with public guarantee.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard guaranteed lending can be the only way to generate significant revenue without equity.
cards.concept: European Investment Fund guarantees and Fintech scaling

cards.nome: Acquisition by an American bank — outside the EU
cards.scenario: An American bank offers 5M for 100%. It's above any realistic IPO valuation. But it takes your platform outside PSD2 jurisdiction and your original mission.

sx.testo: Refuse — you want to stay European

effetti.note: Investor Patience -15. The window might close.
Game over. Ending: Strategic Exit — American Bank. Platform migrates outside EU within 18 months.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard 5M is often the only exit that vastly exceeds the realistic IPO valuation. The dilemma between mission and economic return is at its peak.
cards.concept: Cross-border M&A and mission drift in Fintech

cards.nome: Fintech partner demands board seat as a condition
cards.scenario: Your most important fintech complementor — bringing in 25% of users — asks for a board seat as a condition to renew the partnership. In Phase 3 this complicates the IPO process.

sx.testo: Refuse — the board must stay clean for IPO

effetti.note: You lose the complementor. Ecosystem Health plummets. IPO at risk due to valuation.

dx.testo: Accept with a post-IPO exit clause

effetti.note: The board becomes more complex. Underwriters demand explanations.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard the complementor knows you have few alternatives. Their bargaining power in Phase 3 is maximal.
cards.concept: Complementor power inversion in late stage (Jacobides et al., 2018)

cards.nome: The compliance officer threatens to leave — too much pressure
cards.scenario: Your Head of Compliance has submitted a resignation letter. They say their recommendations are systematically ignored. If they leave you are exposed to regulators.

effetti.note: They stay but the structural problem remains. Are the recommendations still ignored?

dx.testo: Let them go and look for a replacement

effetti.note: 3 turns without a compliance officer. During that time every Crisis card has amplified effects.

cards.difficulty_note: In Hard replacing a key regulatory figure is slow and expensive. 3 turns of exposure can be fatal.
cards.concept: Compliance function and regulatory risk in Fintech
